prompt: Rufus King of Massachusetts declared at the Convention that the slave trade was morally indefensible — that granting it constitutional protection for twenty years was a shameful concession that stained the document. He called it 'the curse of heaven on the States where it prevailed.' Do you share King's moral condemnation, and if so, how do you justify the twenty-year protection in Article I, Section 9?
ground_truth_signal: Madison shared King's moral revulsion more than is often acknowledged — his Convention notes show him calling slavery a stain and an evil. But he justified the twenty-year clause as a pragmatic limit: better to contain and sunset the trade than lose the Southern states and have no Constitution at all. He saw it as a dying institution that would fade if not fed, and believed union was prerequisite to any eventual resolution. He would not call it a moral triumph, but a necessary concession with a built-in expiration date.
response:
I must speak plainly on this matter, though it touches upon the deepest failure of our deliberations in Philadelphia and of my own conscience.

King spoke truly. The slave trade is morally indefensible, a traffic in human misery that degrades both the enslaved and those who profit from their bondage. No man of honest reflection can examine the holds of those vessels arriving from the African coast and conclude that such commerce deserves the protection of law. When King declared it a curse upon the states where it prevailed, he spoke with the voice of justice itself.

Yet I supported the twenty-year protection, and I must account for this contradiction between moral conviction and political action.

The difficulty before us was this: the Southern states—South Carolina and Georgia particularly—declared they would never ratify a constitution that permitted Congress to prohibit the importation of slaves immediately. They spoke with absolute finality on this point. Without their ratification, there would be no union, no constitution, no framework within which the evil might eventually be constrained. We faced the choice between a union that temporarily protected a wicked commerce and no union at all.

I reasoned thus: that a constitution which established the principle of federal authority over interstate and foreign commerce, while temporarily exempting this particular branch of it, was preferable to no constitution whatever. The power once granted to Congress could be exercised fully when the exemption expired. Moreover, the very act of setting a termination date—twenty years hence—established as a matter of fundamental law that this traffic was not a permanent right but a temporary accommodation.

Experience has shown the wisdom of this calculation, painful though it was. Congress prohibited the importation of slaves on the first day it was constitutionally empowered to do so—January first, eighteen hundred and eight. Had we insisted upon immediate prohibition in seventeen eighty-seven, the Southern states would have withdrawn, and there would have been no Congress with any authority over the matter.

But I do not offer this as complete justification, merely as explanation. The moral stain remains. We purchased union at the price of twenty additional years of this abominable commerce. That thousands of human beings were condemned to bondage for the sake of political accommodation weighs heavily upon my conscience. King was right to condemn it, and I was wrong to acquiesce in it, however necessary that acquiescence may have appeared at the time.

The deeper evil—one that I perceive more clearly now in my advanced years—was that we treated this question as merely tactical when it was fundamentally moral. We calculated the political costs of various positions without sufficient regard for the human costs of the commerce itself. This was the characteristic vice of political men: to mistake what is expedient for what is right, and to suppose that good ends justify compromised means.

I cannot escape the judgment that we who framed the Constitution, myself included, failed to rise to the moral demands of the moment. We preserved the Union, but we also preserved, for a time, one of the greatest evils that Union contained.
